steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — not 'esse'
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go) — not 'esse'
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see) — not 'esse'

hints.Ablative: Ablative singular of 1st declension looks the same, but 'Amicitia' is the subject here
hints.Vocative: Cicero is not addressing friendship — it is the subject of the definition

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'amicitia, amicitiae' is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'amicitia' with -a is 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'amicitia' is 1st declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'res' — 'rerum' is genitive plural
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural would be 'res' — 'rerum' is genitive plural
hints.Dative: Dative plural would be 'rebus' — 'rerum' is genitive

hints.1st: 1st declension genitive plural ends in -arum — 'res, rei' is 5th declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension genitive plural ends in -um/-ium — 'rerum' is 5th declension
hints.4th: 4th declension genitive plural ends in -uum — 'rerum' is 5th declension

hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'consensione' — 'consensio' is the predicate nominative
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'consensionem' — 'consensio' is nominative after 'est'

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'consensio, consensionis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'consensio' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us/-u — 'consensio, consensionis' is 3rd declension

steps.target: All things both divine and human — the totality of existence
choices: All things both divine and human — the totality of existence

hints.Only religious rituals: 'Divinarum' includes all divine matters, not just rituals — paired with 'humanarumque' it means everything
hints.Political and military matters only: The phrase encompasses ALL things divine and human, not just politics and war
hints.Only philosophical questions: Cicero's scope is broader — divine and human affairs means the entirety of existence

translation: Friendship is nothing other than agreement on all things divine and human.
